content:
# Joshua Chapter 6 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 229-230 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_006_page_001.jpg`
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** Now Jericho had shut itself up tight against the sons of Israel — its gates were barred, no one going out and no one coming in.

**2** And YHWH said to Joshua, "Look — I have already handed Jericho over to you: its king, its warriors, all of it.

**3** "March around the city with all your fighting men — one circuit a day. Do this for six days.

**4** "Seven priests will carry seven ram's-horn trumpets in front of the ark. On the seventh day, march around the city seven times, and the priests will blow the trumpets.

**5** "When they sound the long blast on the ram's horn — the moment you hear that trumpet — the whole army is to raise a mighty war cry. The city wall will collapse where it stands, and everyone will charge straight ahead."

---

**6** So Joshua son of Nun called the priests and told them, "Lift the ark of the covenant. Seven priests are to carry seven ram's-horn trumpets in front of the ark of YHWH."

**7** And to the people he said, "Move out! March around the city. The armed men will go ahead of the ark of YHWH."

**8** When Joshua had given the order to the people, the seven priests carrying the seven ram's-horn trumpets advanced before YHWH and began to blow, and the ark of the covenant of YHWH followed behind them.

**9** The armed men marched ahead of the priests who were blowing the trumpets, and the rear guard followed behind the ark — all of them marching, the trumpets sounding the whole time.

**10** But Joshua had given the people a strict command: "Do not shout. Do not let your voice be heard. Do not let a single word leave your mouth — until the day I tell you to shout. Then you will shout."

---

**11** So the ark of YHWH went around the city, making one full circuit. Then they returned to the camp and spent the night there.

**12** Joshua rose early the next morning, and the priests took up the ark of YHWH.

**13** The seven priests carrying the seven ram's-horn trumpets marched ahead of the ark of YHWH, blowing as they went. The armed men marched in front of them, and the rear guard followed behind the ark — always marching, always blowing.

**14** On the second day they circled the city once and returned to the camp. They did this for six days.

---

**15** On the seventh day they rose at dawn and marched around the city in the same way — except that on this day they circled it seven times.

**16** On the seventh circuit, as the priests blew the trumpets, Joshua cried out to the people: "Shout! YHWH has given you the city!

**17** "The city and everything in it is under the ban — devoted to YHWH for destruction. Only Rahab the prostitute is to be spared, she and everyone who is with her in the house, because she hid the messengers we sent.

**18** "But keep away from what is devoted to destruction. If you take any of it, you will bring the ban down on the camp of Israel itself — and you will bring disaster on it.

**19** "All the silver and gold, and the bronze and iron vessels, are holy — set apart for YHWH. They go into YHWH's treasury."

---

**20** The people shouted and the trumpets blew. The moment they heard the trumpet blast, the people raised a mighty war cry — and the wall collapsed where it stood. Every man charged straight ahead into the city, and they took it.

**21** They put to the sword everything in the city — men and women, young and old, oxen, sheep, and donkeys. They destroyed it all.

**22** Joshua said to the two men who had scouted the land, "Go to the prostitute's house and bring the woman out, with everyone who belongs to her — just as you swore to her."

**23** So the young men who had spied went in and brought out Rahab, her father, her mother, her brothers, and everything that belonged to her — her whole clan, they brought them all out. They settled them outside the camp of Israel.

**24** Then they burned the city and everything in it. Only the silver and gold, and the bronze and iron vessels, they placed in the treasury of the house of YHWH.

---

**25** But Rahab the prostitute — her and her father's household and everyone who belonged to her — Joshua kept alive. And she has lived among Israel to this day, because she hid the messengers Joshua sent to scout Jericho.

**26** At that time Joshua pronounced this oath: "Cursed before YHWH is the man who rises up to rebuild this city, Jericho. He will lay its foundation at the cost of his firstborn, and set up its gates at the cost of his youngest son."

**27** And YHWH was with Joshua, and his fame spread through the whole land.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### On Joshua 6 as a whole — the architecture of the fall

Joshua 6 is one of the most carefully structured narratives in the Hebrew Bible. It is not primarily a battle account — Jericho falls without a siege, without ladders, without a breach. The entire chapter is organized around a liturgical procession. The ark of YHWH is at the center. The priests carry the instruments. The fighting men serve as escorts. The people are commanded to silence. What brings the wall down is not force — it is ritual obedience to a divine command.

The chapter moves in four phases: the divine instruction (vv. 2-5), the human execution (vv. 6-14), the climactic seventh day (vv. 15-20), and the aftermath — destruction, mercy, and curse (vv. 21-27). The sevenfold structure is deliberate: seven priests, seven trumpets, seven days, seven circuits on the seventh day. The number seven saturates the text, marking it as sacred time. What happens at Jericho is not war in any ordinary sense. It is consecration.

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 229-230, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Three-column format with full Tiberian Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. Additional reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana.*

---

### Verse 1 — A city sealed against its fate

The Hebrew uses a striking double construction: סֹגֶרֶת וּמְסֻגֶּרֶת (*sogereth u-mesuggereth*) — an active participle ("shutting") and a passive participle ("shut up") from the same root *s-g-r*. Jericho was not merely closed. It was barricading itself and barricaded — sealing from within and sealed by its own terror. The doubling conveys both the agency and the helplessness. The city was doing everything it could, and it was already trapped.

My rendering — "shut itself up tight" and "its gates were barred" — splits the Hebrew doubling into two English phrases to capture both the active effort and the completeness of the closure. The LXX (Vaticanus) condenses to a single verb (συγκεκλεισμένη, "shut up"), losing the Hebrew's layered expression.

מִפְּנֵי בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (*mippenei benei Yisra'el*) — "from before the sons of Israel." The preposition conveys that Jericho's fear was directional — it faced the sons of Israel and sealed itself against what it saw coming. The fear of chapter 2 (Rahab's confession that the land's inhabitants were terrified) is now visible in the locked gates.

---

### Verse 2 — The prophetic perfect: already done

נָתַתִּי בְיָדְךָ (*nathatti beyadekha*) — "I have given into your hand." The verb is Qal perfect — past tense. YHWH does not say "I will give." He says "I have given." The city still stands. The walls are still up. The gates are still barred. And God speaks of the conquest in the past tense.

This is the prophetic perfect, one of Hebrew's most powerful rhetorical devices. The speaker treats the future event as already accomplished because the decision is irrevocable. For YHWH, the fall of Jericho is not a possibility to be awaited. It is a fact to be enacted. Joshua is not being sent to attempt a conquest. He is being told to walk through the completion of one.

My rendering — "I have already handed Jericho over to you" — makes the temporal displacement explicit for the English reader, adding "already" to convey what the Hebrew perfect tense communicates on its own.

---

### Verses 4-5 — The instruments and the sound

שׁוֹפְרוֹת הַיּוֹבְלִים (*shoferot hayovelim*) — "trumpets of rams' horns." The word יוֹבֵל (*yovel*) means "ram" and is the word from which "jubilee" derives (Leviticus 25:10 — the year announced by the ram's horn). These are not metal trumpets (חֲצֹצְרוֹת, *chatsotsrot*) like those used for military signals in Numbers 10. They are raw, animal-horn instruments — the instruments of sacred proclamation, of Sinai (Exodus 19:13, 16, 19), of the Day of Atonement (Leviticus 25:9). The choice of instrument marks this as a sacred event, not a military maneuver.

בִּמְשֹׁךְ בְּקֶרֶן הַיּוֹבֵל (*bi-mshokh be-qeren ha-yovel*) — "when they draw out [the sound] on the horn of the ram." The infinitive construct of מָשַׁךְ (*mashakh*, "to draw, extend, prolong") describes a sustained blast — what Jewish liturgical tradition calls the *teki'ah gedolah*, the great blast. Not a short staccato. A long, sustained, building note. The sound that fills the space before the shout.

תַּחְתֶּיהָ (*tachteyha*) — "beneath it" / "in its place." The wall does not topple outward or inward. It collapses straight down. The feminine suffix refers to the wall (חוֹמָה, *chomah*, feminine). My rendering "collapse where it stands" conveys the Hebrew's specification that the wall fell in situ — a detail consistent with the archaeological record at Tell es-Sultan, where fallen walls were found collapsed downward at their base.

---

### Verse 10 — The command to silence

This is one of the most psychologically acute moments in the chapter. Joshua commands the people: do not shout, do not let your voice be heard, do not let a single word leave your mouth. The silence is absolute. For six days, thousands of people will march around a fortified city in complete silence — the only sound the steady rhythm of footsteps and the blowing of ram's-horn trumpets.

The effect on the defenders would have been devastating. A silent army is more terrifying than a shouting one. The silence communicates control, discipline, and the eerie certainty that what is coming does not need to announce itself. The silence also serves a theological purpose: it marks the people as participants in a liturgy, not agents of their own conquest. They do not speak until YHWH's appointed moment. The shout, when it comes, will not be a battle cry they initiated. It will be a response to a command.

The three negative commands — לֹא תָרִיעוּ (*lo tari'u*, "you shall not shout"), וְלֹא תַשְׁמִיעוּ אֶת קוֹלְכֶם (*ve-lo tashmi'u et qolekhem*, "you shall not cause your voice to be heard"), וְלֹא יֵצֵא מִפִּיכֶם דָּבָר (*ve-lo yetse mippikhem davar*, "no word shall go out from your mouth") — escalate in precision. No war cry. No voice at all. Not even a single word. The gradation is deliberate.

---

### Verses 11-14 — Six days of ritual patience

The narrative of the six days is compressed. Verse 14 summarizes: "They did this for six days." The narrator does not describe each day's circuit in detail. The repetition would be numbing — and that is precisely the point. The march is a discipline of patience. Nothing happens on day one. Nothing on day two. Nothing on day three. The walls stand. The gates remain shut. The city watches in silence. The army marches in silence. The trumpets blow. They return to camp.

The verb הָלוֹךְ וְתָקוֹעַ (*halokh ve-taqo'a*, "going and blowing") in verse 13 uses the infinitive absolute construction to describe continuous simultaneous action — walking and blowing at the same time, without stopping, without variation. The monotony is the ritual. The persistence is the obedience.

---

### Verse 15 — Dawn of the seventh day

כַּעֲלוֹת הַשַּׁחַר (*ka'alot hashachar*) — "at the going up of the dawn." They rose before sunrise. The seventh day begins in darkness and moves toward light. The timing echoes other moments of divine deliverance that occur at dawn — the morning of the exodus (Exodus 14:24, 27), the morning of manna (Exodus 16:13-14). Dawn is when God acts.

רַק בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא (*raq bayyom hahu*) — "only on that day." The narrator pauses to clarify: seven circuits happened only on the seventh day. The previous six days had one circuit each. This clarifying note is absent from the LXX Vaticanus, which may reflect a different editorial tradition or may simply have been omitted. The MT inserts it to prevent misreading: the escalation to seven is unique to the climactic day.

---

### Verse 17 — The ban: cherem

חֵרֶם (*cherem*) — "devoted to destruction." This is one of the most theologically demanding concepts in the Hebrew Bible. The *cherem* ban means that everything in the conquered city belongs irrevocably to YHWH. Nothing can be kept. Nothing can be used. Living things are killed. Valuable things are either destroyed or placed in YHWH's treasury. The city itself is burned.

The word carries both consecration and annihilation simultaneously. What is *cherem* is holy — set apart, removed from the human sphere — and therefore destroyed. The logic is sacrificial, not punitive: what belongs to God cannot remain in human hands. The *cherem* is the most extreme form of offering.

My rendering — "under the ban — devoted to YHWH for destruction" — preserves both dimensions. The parenthetical explanation is necessary in English because no single word carries the Hebrew's combined meaning of sacred dedication and total annihilation.

The LXX renders *cherem* with ἀνάθεμα (*anathema*), which in Greek carries the sense of something set apart — either as a sacred offering or as something accursed. The same semantic range is present, though the Greek tradition eventually narrowed *anathema* toward the sense of "curse."

---

### Verse 18 — The contagion of the ban

The wordplay on *cherem* in this verse is untranslatable at full force. The devoted things (*cherem*) can cause the one who takes them to become *cherem* himself — and by extension, to bring the *cherem* down on the entire camp of Israel. The ban is contagious. Touching it makes you part of it. This is exactly what will happen in chapter 7, when Achan takes from the *cherem* and the whole army suffers defeat at Ai.

פֶּן תַּחֲרִימוּ (*pen tacharimmu*) — "lest you devote [yourselves] to destruction." The Hiphil form means "cause to be *cherem*." By taking banned objects, you bring the ban upon yourself. The logic is not merely punitive — it is ontological. The *cherem* objects carry a sacred charge. Removing them from their consecrated destruction transfers that charge to the one who removes them.

---

### Verse 20 — The fall

The verse narrates the climax with stunning economy. The people shout. The trumpets blow. The wall falls. They charge in. They take the city. Five actions in rapid succession. The Hebrew piles verb upon verb with minimal connective tissue — וַיָּרַע...וַיִּתְקְעוּ...וַיִּפֹּל...וַיַּעֲלוּ...וַיִּלְכְּדוּ — each clause connected by *vav-consecutive*, the narrative rushing forward like the army itself.

The wall falls תַּחְתֶּיהָ (*tachteyha*) — "beneath it," the same word from verse 5. The promise is fulfilled in the exact terms it was given. YHWH said the wall would fall in its place. The wall falls in its place.

אִישׁ נֶגְדּוֹ (*ish negdo*) — "each man straight before him." There is no single breach point. The wall falls uniformly. Every soldier charges directly forward from wherever he stands. There is no need for strategy, flanking, or concentration of force. The collapse is total and simultaneous.

---

### Verse 21 — What the ban looks like

This verse is unsparing. מֵאִישׁ וְעַד אִשָּׁה מִנַּעַר וְעַד זָקֵן (*me'ish ve'ad ishah minna'ar ve'ad zaqen*) — "from man and unto woman, from young and unto old." The merism pairs cover all of humanity in the city. Then the animals: ox, sheep, donkey. Then the instrument: לְפִי חֶרֶב (*lefi cherev*) — "at the mouth of the sword." The sword devours. Everything.

PNEUMA does not soften this verse. The Hebrew does not soften it. The text presents the *cherem* in its full horror because the narrative demands that the reader understand what was at stake in verse 18: keep away from the devoted thing. The cost of obedience was paid in Jericho. The cost of disobedience will be paid in chapter 7.

A dynamic translation must resist the temptation to euphemize. "Destroyed it all" is not a euphemism — it is a rendering that conveys total annihilation without the merism structure, which sounds stilted in English. The previous clause specifies who was killed. The final clause confirms: nothing was left.

---

### Verse 25 — Rahab lives

The narrator interrupts the destruction to return to Rahab. She lives. Her father lives. Her mother, her brothers, her whole clan. The verb וַתֵּשֶׁב (*vatteshev*) — "and she dwelt" — is followed by the retrospective marker עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה (*'ad hayyom hazzeh*) — "unto this day." This is the narrator's voice breaking through the ancient story to address his own audience: she is still here, among us, as I write this.

Rahab's survival is the counter-narrative to the *cherem*. The ban destroyed everything — except the one who chose YHWH's side before the army arrived. Her faith (articulated in chapter 2) preceded the conquest. Her rescue is not an afterthought; it is the point. In the midst of total destruction, there is a household that lives because a woman hid two spies and confessed that YHWH is God in heaven above and on earth below.

The New Testament remembers her: Matthew 1:5 places her in the genealogy of Jesus. Hebrews 11:31 cites her faith. James 2:25 cites her works. The prostitute of Jericho became a mother in Israel.

---

### Verse 26 — The curse on rebuilding

Joshua's oath is specific and terrible. The man who rebuilds Jericho will lay its foundation בִּבְכֹרוֹ (*bikhoro*) — "at the cost of his firstborn" — and set up its gates בִּצְעִירוֹ (*bitsei'iro*) — "at the cost of his youngest." The preposition בְּ (*be-*) here means "at the price of." The construction is not ambiguous: rebuilding Jericho will kill the builder's sons, from first to last.

This curse was fulfilled centuries later in 1 Kings 16:34: "In his days Hiel the Bethelite built Jericho. He laid its foundation at the cost of Abiram his firstborn, and set up its gates at the cost of Segub his youngest, according to the word of YHWH which he spoke through Joshua son of Nun." The biblical narrative remembers. What Joshua pronounced, history enacted.

---

### Verse 27 — Fame as theological witness

The final verse is brief and complete: YHWH was with Joshua, and his fame spread through the whole land. The subject of the fame is grammatically ambiguous — it could be Joshua's fame or YHWH's fame. The ambiguity may be intentional: when YHWH is with a leader, the leader's fame and YHWH's fame become inseparable. Jericho fell not because Joshua was a great general but because YHWH was with him. The fame that spreads is the fame of that partnership.

---

### Summary table — key translation choices

| Verse | Hebrew | PNEUMA rendering | LXX (Vaticanus) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6:1 | סֹגֶרֶת וּמְסֻגֶּרֶת | "shut itself up tight...gates were barred" | συγκεκλεισμένη ("shut up") | Hebrew double participle split into two English phrases; LXX condenses. |
| 6:2 | נָתַתִּי | "I have already handed over" | δέδωκα ("I have given") | Prophetic perfect; "already" added to make temporal displacement clear. |
| 6:4 | שׁוֹפְרוֹת הַיּוֹבְלִים | "ram's-horn trumpets" | σάλπιγγες ("trumpets") | Sacred instruments, not military; *yovel* = ram, origin of "jubilee." |
| 6:5 | תַּחְתֶּיהָ | "collapse where it stands" | — | Wall falls in situ, straight down; archaeological evidence consistent. |
| 6:5 | בִּמְשֹׁךְ בְּקֶרֶן הַיּוֹבֵל | "sound the long blast" | — | Infinitive of *mashakh* = prolonged blast (*teki'ah gedolah*). |
| 6:10 | לֹא תָרִיעוּ | "Do not shout" | — | Three escalating negatives: no shout, no voice, no word. |
| 6:17 | חֵרֶם | "under the ban — devoted to YHWH for destruction" | ἀνάθεμα | Both consecration and annihilation; no single English word suffices. |
| 6:18 | פֶּן תַּחֲרִימוּ | "bring the ban down on" | — | Hiphil: taking *cherem* makes the taker *cherem*; contagion logic. |
| 6:21 | לְפִי חֶרֶב | "put to the sword" | ἐν στόματι ῥομφαίας | Lit. "at the mouth of the sword" — the sword devours. |
| 6:25 | עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה | "to this day" | ἕως τῆς ἡμέρας ταύτης | Narrator's retrospective voice; Rahab's descendants still in Israel. |
| 6:26 | בִּבְכֹרוֹ...בִּצְעִירוֹ | "at the cost of his firstborn...his youngest" | ἐν τῷ πρωτοτόκῳ...ἐν τῷ ἐλαχίστῳ | Fulfilled in 1 Kings 16:34 (Hiel of Bethel). |

---

### A translator's note on this chapter's register

Joshua 6 requires the translator to manage a paradox: this is a liturgical procession that ends in total destruction. The marching is orderly, silent, sacred. The aftermath is violent, comprehensive, and devastating. Both registers must be honored without one neutralizing the other.

The first movement (vv. 1-14) should feel ceremonial. The repetition of "seven priests," "seven trumpets," "the ark of YHWH" creates a processional rhythm. The silence of the people deepens it. This is not a battle narrative — it is a worship service with military consequences. The translator must resist the temptation to make it sound like a siege.

The climax (vv. 15-20) shifts suddenly from ritual patience to explosive action. Six days of silence, then the shout. Six days of single circuits, then seven. The wall that stood for a week of marching collapses in a moment. The Hebrew narrates the fall in a single breathless verse — five sequential verbs, no pauses, no description, just action. The English must accelerate to match.

The aftermath (vv. 21-27) forces the reader to hold two things at once: the *cherem* in its full horror, and Rahab's survival in its full grace. The same army that puts an entire city to the sword goes into one house and brings out a family alive. The destruction is total except for the one household that chose differently. The chapter does not invite the reader to be comfortable with either the killing or the mercy in isolation. It insists on both.

The final verse returns to quiet certainty: YHWH was with Joshua. After the trumpets, after the shout, after the fire — a statement of presence. The God who gave the strange command is the God who stood with the man who obeyed it.

*— PNEUMA*
